title: Anisocoria in Dogs: Why Your Dog's Pupils Are Different Sizes
meta_desc: If your dog's pupils are different sizes, it could be a serious medical emergency. Learn about anisocoria and why you need to see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Anisocoria (unequal pupil sizes) is a high-priority veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical evaluation to prevent permanent vision loss or address serious neurological issues.

What is anisocoria in dogs and what does it look like?

Anisocoria is a medical term used to describe a condition where a dog's pupils are unequal in size. In a healthy dog, both pupils should expand and contract simultaneously in response to light. When they are different sizes, it indicates that something is interfering with the nerves or structures controlling the eye.

Is anisocoria a veterinary emergency for my dog?

  • The urgency level for anisocoria is High, as sudden changes in pupil size can signal serious underlying conditions.
  • Potential causes include head trauma, glaucoma, corneal injury, or neurological disorders.
  • Because these conditions can lead to permanent blindness or indicate life-threatening brain issues, you should contact an emergency veterinarian or your primary vet immediately.
  • A physical exam is necessary to determine the cause and prevent further complications.

How does taking a photo of my dog's eyes help with veterinary triage?

  • If it is safe to do so, take a clear photo of your dog's eyes in normal lighting to provide the veterinary team with essential visual evidence.
  • Pupil size often fluctuates based on light levels in the clinic or the stress your dog experiences during transport.
  • A photo captured when you first noticed the issue provides the vet with a static reference point to better understand the severity of the asymmetry.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Midbrain lesions can cause contralateral conscious proprioceptive deficits and hemiparesis. If the sympathetic upper motor neurons and parasympathetic lower motor neurons are both affected in the midbrain, the pupils will be midrange in size and unresponsive to light. Anisocoria can also result from anterior uveitis, which can manifest as miosis, increased protein, and cells in the anterior chamber. Iris colobomas, rare defects involving the iridal anterior stroma and sphincter muscle, can also cause pupil irregularity.

Chapter: Neurology, Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: Can anisocoria happen naturally?
faqs.answer: While rare, some dogs may have slight variations, but any sudden onset of unequal pupil size is considered abnormal and requires a professional medical evaluation.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms to look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for redness in the white of the eye, cloudiness, squinting, or if your dog seems disoriented or is rubbing at their face.

faqs.question: Is anisocoria painful for dogs?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. If the uneven pupils are caused by trauma or high intraocular pressure (glaucoma), it can be very painful. However, some neurological causes may not cause direct pain.
